coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of multifunctional carbon dots from Crataegi Fructus for pH sensing, cell imaging and hemostatic effects
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Rui
Guo, Yufan
Luo, Fazhen
Yang, Tianshu
Zhou, Zhenhua
Wang, Zhengtao
Xie, Yan
coredata.dc:description: Multifunctional carbon dots with intrinsic biological activity and fluorescent properties exhibit tremendous potential for applications in various fields, especially in the biomedical field. This paper reports a green and facile method for the synthesis of multifunctional carbon dots (CFCDs) via pyrolysis using the herbal medicine Crataegi Fructus as a precursor, and the fluorescent properties and biological activities of CFCDs are investigated, while the corresponding mechanisms are explored. The obtained CFCDs present a graphitic structure with an average particle size of 2.35 nm, abundant functional groups on their surface, and possess high aqueous solubility and excellent stability. Furthermore, under acidic conditions, these CFCDs have pH-dependent fluorescence properties based on protonation and deprotonation, showing a high linear correlation with pH values ranging from 0.5 to 4.5. The low cytotoxicity of CFCDs and their efficient internalization by cells make them ideal for cell imaging. More importantly, CFCDs exert good hemostatic effects through the extrinsic and common pathway and promote platelet aggregation. In conclusion, CFCDs can simultaneously realize pH sensing, cell imaging, and hemorrhagic disease treatment and, thus, have potential for wide application in medical domain.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Crataegi Fructus
Pyrolysis
Hemostatic effect
pH sensing
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of bleeding time (BT) and clotting time (CT) in mice
Coagulation assays of CFCDs in vitro
Cytotoxicity and cell imaging of CFCDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 81873198 ) and the Academic Leader Program of Shanghai Municipal Health Commission ( 2022XD026 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of CFCDs. (b) HRTEM images of CFCDs. (c) Size distribution of CFCDs. (d) Fluorescence excitation (black line), emission (red line), and UV–vis absorption (blue line) spectra of CFCDs. Inset: photographs of the aqueous CFCD solution under visible light (left) and 365 nm UV light irradiation (right). (e) Fluorescence emission spectra of CFCDs at excitation wavelengths from 310 to 490 nm. (f) FTIR spectrum of CFCDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) XPS survey scan, (b) C1s XPS, (c) N1s XPS, and (d) O1s XPS spectra of CFCDs.
Fluorescence emission intensity of CFCDs as a function of (a) ionic concentration and (b) pH values ranging from 2 to 12.

(a) The fluorescence spectrum of CFCDs with pH values from 0.5 to 4.5 under a 350 nm excitation wavelength. (b) Linear correlations between the fluorescence intensity and pH values from 0.5 to 4.5. (c) Fluorescence reversibility against pH change between 0.5 and 4.5, repeatedly.
Cell viability of (a) MCF-7, (b) MDA-MB-231, and (c) Caco-2 cells after treatment with different concentrations of CFCDs for 24 h.
Confocal laser micrographs of (a) MCF-7, (b) MDA-MB-231, and (c) Caco-2 cells after incubation with 1 mg/mL CFCDs for 2 h. Scale bar = 100, 50, 100 µm, respectively.

Effects of CFCDs on (a) bleeding time and (b) clotting time in mice. The ICR mice were divided into a control group (treated with saline, NS), positive group (treated with Yunnan Baiyao, YNBY), and low-, medium-, and high-dose CFCD groups. **p < 0.01 compared with the control group; ##p < 0.01 and #p < 0.05 compared with the positive group (n = 10).

Effects of different concentrations of CFCDs on (a) prothrombin time (PT), (b) activated partial thromboplastin time (APTT), (c) thrombin (TT), and (d) platelet aggregation rate. ***p < 0.001, **p < 0.01, and *p < 0.05 compared with the control group (n = 6).
Schematic illustration of the synthesis and purification of CFCDs.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of multifunctional carbon dots from Crataegi Fructus for pH sensing, cell imaging and hemostatic effects

Research Center for Health and Nutrition, Shanghai University of Traditional Chinese Medicine, 1200 Cailun Road, Shanghai 201203, China (Y. Xie).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai University of Traditional Chinese Medicine

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multifunctional carbon dots with intrinsic biological activity and fluorescent properties exhibit tremendous potential for applications in various fields, especially in the biomedical field. This paper reports a green and facile method for the synthesis of multifunctional carbon dots (CFCDs) via pyrolysis using the herbal medicine Crataegi Fructus as a precursor, and the fluorescent properties and biological activities of CFCDs are investigated, while the corresponding mechanisms are explored. The obtained CFCDs present a graphitic structure with an average particle size of 2.35 nm, abundant functional groups on their surface, and possess high aqueous solubility and excellent stability. Furthermore, under acidic conditions, these CFCDs have pH-dependent fluorescence properties based on protonation and deprotonation, showing a high linear correlation with pH values ranging from 0.5 to 4.5. The low cytotoxicity of CFCDs and their efficient internalization by cells make them ideal for cell imaging. More importantly, CFCDs exert good hemostatic effects through the extrinsic and common pathway and promote platelet aggregation. In conclusion, CFCDs can simultaneously realize pH sensing, cell imaging, and hemorrhagic disease treatment and, thus, have potential for wide application in medical domain.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a class of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials with diameters of less than 10 nm [1]. With the properties of photostability, high aqueous solubility, biocompatibility, excellent fluorescence (FL) and low cost, CDs have been applied to fluorescent probes [2,3], bioimaging [4–6], biosensing [7,8], photodynamic therapy [9,10], etc. In addition, CDs have been recently reported as therapeutic agents because they also possess some biological activities such as antibacterial activity [11,12], anti-inflammatory activity [13,14], anticancer activity [15,16], and antioxidant activity [17]. CDs with intrinsic biological activity and fluorescent properties are generally termed multifunctional CDs. To date, these CDs are becoming a hot spot in the CDs research field as they can integrate diagnostics and therapeutics for realizing optimized efficacy and safety of comprehensive regimens. For example, multifunctional CDs derived from ginsenoside Re can be taken up by cells for bioimaging to diagnose the presence of tumors, inhibit cancer cell proliferation and induce apoptosis with little toxicity to healthy cells [18]. Therefore, developing multifunctional CDs is potentially valuable for building a more convenient and efficient platform for sensing, imaging, and treatment, which would promote the integration of diagnosis and treatment in the clinic.

To obtain the abovementioned multifunctional CDs, selecting biologically active materials as precursors and functionally modifying the CDs are considered as feasible strategies [19,20]. With the rise of green chemistry, many natural materials or natural renewable resource products have been used as precursors to synthesize multifunctional CDs through hydrothermal, microwave-assisted, pyrolysis, etc., which is called the green synthesis of CDs since these methods are environmentally friendly, nontoxic, and do not require excessive passivation [21,22]. Herbal medicines, as a class of bioactive and renewable natural products, have been used for the green synthesis of CDs because their abundant active ingredients and microelements can provide surface functional groups and heteroatoms, which endow these synthetic CDs the appropriate FL characteristics and good biological activity for disease treatment without extra functional modification or drug loading [23]. Thus, herbal medicines are ideal precursors for the green synthesis of multifunctional CDs.

Crataegi Fructus (CF), the fruit of Crataegus pinnatifida Bge var. major N.E.Br. or Crataegus pinnatifida Bge. (Rosaceae family), is recognized as an herbal remedy for the digestive system and for cardiovascular diseases in Asian countries [24,25]. Its charcoal processed product obtained by carbonizing CF at high temperature, Crataegi Fructus Carbonisata (CFC), has been used to clinically treat chronic colitis and ulcerative colitis for a long time in China [26]. Interestingly, we coincidently found that the carbonization process of CF to prepare CFC was quite similar to the synthesis of CDs from natural products. In addition, the rich carbohydrates, protein, mineral elements, and various active ingredients in CF definitely can afford abundant carbon sources and heteroatoms, demonstrating that CF is a potential precursor for the preparation of multifunctional CDs. Therefore, we speculate that CF-derived CDs will be multifunctional CDs that will possess biological activities analogous to those of CFC. However, to the best of our knowledge, no reports on the synthesis of multifunctional CDs using the herbal medicine CF as a precursor are available.

Herein, we synthesized CF-derived multifunctional CDs (CFCDs) with intrinsic biological activity and fluorescent properties through a facile and green pyrolysis method. Furthermore, the particle size, elemental composition, surface functional groups, and optical properties of CFCDs were characterized and their pH sensitivity, cytotoxicity, and cell imaging capabilities were investigated. In addition, the hemostatic activity of the CFCDs was evaluated and the related mechanisms were explored.

Crataegi Fructus was provided by Shanghai Kangqiao Chinese Medicine Tablet Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was purchased from Gibco Laboratory (Grand Island, New York, USA). Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) was purchased from Thermo Fisher Biochemical Products (Waltham, Massachusetts, USA). 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-5-(3-carboxymethoxyphenyl)-2-(4-sulfophenyl)-2H-tetrazolium (MTS) was provided by Promega Biological Products Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Adenosine 5′-diphosphate (ADP) was purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Yunnan Baiyao capsules were obtained from Yunnan Baiyao Group Co., Ltd (Kunming, China). Besides, all chemicals were of analytical grade and were used without further purification.

Female and male ICR mice (18–22 g) and male Sprague-Dawley (SD) rats (300–350 g) were housed in a controlled environment at room temperature of 24.0 ± 1.0 °C and a 12 h light/dark cycle. All animal procedures were performed in accordance with the Guidelines for Care and Use of Laboratory Animals and approved by the Committee of Ethics of Animal Experimentation of Shanghai University of Traditional Chinese Medicine (No.: PZSHUTCM200522002).

As shown in Scheme 1 , CFCDs were synthesized through a pyrolysis method [27]. Briefly, the dried CF was placed into a crucible with good sealing conditions and then heated using a muffle furnace (SX2 4-100, Shanghai Yangguang Experimental Instruments Co., Ltd., China) for 1 h at 400 °C. After cooling to room temperature, the carbonized CF was further pulverized into a fine powder. Afterward, the fine powder (30 g) was dispersed in 600 mL deionized water (DW) and extracted twice in a water bath at 100 °C for 1 h each. The resulting suspension was prefiltered through filter paper, concentrated under reduced pressure, and then filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. The resulting filtrate was subsequently purified by dialysis (MW: 1000 Da) against DW for 24 h. Finally, the solution was centrifuged at 20,000×g for 0.5 h, 100,000×g for 1.5 h, and 200,000×g for 3 h with an ultracentrifuge instrument (Optima XPN-100 Ultracentrifuge, Beckman Coulter, Inc., USA) to obtain the purified CFCDs. The CFCD solution was kept at 4 °C for later use.

A transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100F, Japan Electron Optics Laboratory, Japan) was used to obtain TEM and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of CFCDs. A Raman spectrometer (Renishaw inVia Qontor, UK) was used to obtain Raman spectra of CFCDs with an excitation wavelength of 532 nm. The ultraviolet-visible (UV-vis) absorption spectra of CFCDs were acquired by a UV-vis spectrophotometer (Agilent 8453, Agilent Technologies Inc., USA). The FL spectra of the CFCDs was measured by an FL spectrophotometer (F-4500, Hitachi, Ltd, Japan). The elemental composition and binding energies of CFCDs were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Axis Supra, Kratos, UK). A Nicolet 380 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was used to acquire the FTIR spectra of the CFCDs over a range of 4000–400 cm−1.

The QY of CFCDs was determined using quinine sulfate (QY = 54 %) dissolved in 0.1 mol/L H2SO4 solution as a reference according to the following equation:
(1)
QY
S
=
QY
R
×
I S
I R
×
A R
A S
×
η S
2
η R
2
In the equation, I is the integrated FL intensity, η is the refractive index of the solvent, and R and S represent the standard and tested samples, respectively.

The pH values (0.5–12) of the CFCD solution (0.1 mg/mL) were adjusted by using 1 M HCl or NaOH with a pH meter (S210, Mettler Toledo, USA). Then the FL emission spectra of CFCDs at different pH values were determined at an excitation wavelength of 352 nm. All measurements were performed in triplicate.

The MTS assay was used to investigate the cytotoxicity of the CFCDs. In brief, the human epithelial cell line Caco-2, and the human breast cancer cell lines MDA-MB-231 and MCF-7 were seeded at a density of 1 × 105 cells per well in a 96-well plate and cultured for 24 h in a 37 °C incubator with a 5 % CO2 atmosphere. The cells were then treated with DMEM containing CFCDs (0.4–2.0 mg/mL, 100 μL) and cultured for 24 h. Subsequently, 120 μL MTS solution (MTS:DMEM = 1:5) was added to each well and incubated for 1 h. The absorbance at 490 nm was measured using a Multiskan Spectrum microplate reader (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA).

For cell imaging experiments, Caco-2, MCF-7, and MDA-MB-231 cells (1.0 × 105 cells) were cultured for 24 h in coverglass bottom dishes. Then, the cells were incubated with 1 mg/mL CFCDs (in DMEM) for 2 h after removing the medium. Finally, the cells were washed 3 times with PBS buffer to eliminate the excess CFCDs. The FL images were collected on a TCSSP8 confocal laser-scanning microscope (CLSM, Leica, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of bleeding time (BT) and clotting time (CT) in mice
ce:section.ce:para: Fifty ICR mice (18–22 g) were randomly and equally divided into 5 groups (5 females and 5 males in each group): control group (normal saline (NS), i.g.), positive group (Yunnan Baiyao, 0.375 g/kg, i.g.), and low-, medium-, and high-dose CFCD groups (1.25, 2.50, and 5.00 mg/kg respectively, i.g.) to evaluate the effect of CFCDs on BT. Mice were dosed once daily for 7 consecutive days. Determination of BT using a modified tail bleeding model one hour after the final gavage dose [28]. Specifically, after the mice were anesthetized by pentobarbital, the tails of the mice were transversely cut off with sterile surgical scissors at a position 3 mm from the tip. Subsequently, the blood from the wound was blotted with filter paper every 30 s. The time from tail clipping to wound hemostasis (no blood on the filter paper) was recorded (BT).

To evaluate the effect of CFCDs on CT in mice, the mice were grouped and administered according to the BT assay conditions described above. One hour after the last dose, blood was collected from the fundus venous plexus through a capillary tube after mice were anesthetized. The capillary tube was broken from both sides every 15 s, and the CT was recorded immediately when the appearance of hemagglutination silk was observed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Coagulation assays of CFCDs in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Male SD rats (300–350 g) were anesthetized with pentobarbital after fasting for 8 h. Subsequently, a vacuum tube containing sodium citrate (3.8 %, 1:9 v/v) was used to collect fresh whole blood from the abdominal aorta. Then, the resulting anticoagulated blood was centrifuged for 10 min at 3000 rpm. The obtained plasma (200 μL) and CFCD solution (50 μL) at concentrations of 30, 90, and 180 μg/mL were mixed in a test cup and incubated for 3 min at 37 °C. Then, prothrombin time (PT), thrombin time (TT), and activated partial thromboplastin time (APTT) were assayed using a fully automatic coagulation analyzer (CS-5100, Sysmex Corporation, Japan). All coagulation assays were performed in six replicates.

Fresh whole blood was obtained as described in Section 1. Subsequently, platelet-rich plasma (PRP) was obtained by centrifugation of whole blood at 1000 rpm for 10 min. The remaining blood samples were centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min to obtain platelet-poor plasma (PPP), which was used as a blank to adjust the platelet count of PRP to 1.5 × 108 cells/mL using PPP.

The platelet aggregation rate was determined via a platelet aggregator (AggRAM™, Helena Biosciences, UK). PRP (215 μL) was stirred in an aggregometer cuvette at 37 °C, and 10 µL of NS (control) or different concentrations of CFCD solution (30, 90, and 180 μg/mL) were added. After incubation for 3 min, 25 µL of ADP (10 μM) was added to trigger platelet aggregation. The maximum aggregation rate was recorded after 300 s of monitoring platelet aggregation at 650 nm.
The results are presented as the mean ± standard deviation. Statistical analysis was performed by Student’s t test, and p values below 0.05 and 0.01 were considered significant differences.

The size distribution and morphology structure of the CFCDs were determined by TEM. Fig. 1 a and 1c show that CFCDs with spherical shapes exhibit good dispersion and have a homogeneous size distribution with an average particle size of 2.35 ± 0.67 nm. Obvious CFCD lattice fringes can be observed with a spacing of 0.21 nm (Fig. 1b), corresponding to the diffraction plane of graphite (100), which indicates that it has a distinct graphitic crystal structure [29]. The Raman spectrum of CFCDs displays two characteristic peaks at 1360 cm−1 and 1581 cm−1 (Fig. S1), corresponding to the D band associated with the vibration of sp3 hybridized carbon at the edge of the crystal and the G band reflecting the in-plane vibration of the conjugated sp2 hybridized carbon, respectively [30,31]. The intensity ratio of D band and G band (ID/IG) is 0.77, indicating that CFCDs possess graphitic crystalline structure along with the surface defects [32]. In addition, the UV-vis spectrum of the CFCDs exhibits an absorption peak at 250–300 nm (Fig. 1d), which is associated with the π-π* transition in the sp2 C domain [17,33]. In addition, the FL excitation and emission spectra of CFCDs shown in Fig. 1d demonstrate that its optimal excitation wavelength is 352 nm (λex), and the emission spectrum has a maximum absorption at 445 nm (λem). As shown in the inset of Fig. 1d, the resulting CFCDs appeared as a yellowish transparent solution under natural light, while they exhibited a bright blue FL when exposed to UV light at 365 nm. Moreover, Fig. 1e shows that CFCDs exhibit excitation-dependent FL emission. With the increase in excitation wavelength from 310 nm to 490 nm, the intensity of the emission spectrum of CFCDs showed a trend of increasing first and then decreasing, accompanied by a red-shift of the emission peak, which is attributed to different emission traps on the surface of CFCDs and quantum effects [34]. Furthermore, the QY of CFCDs was calculated to be 6.01 %, which is comparable with the previously reported QY of CDs synthesized using pyrolysis [35].

The composition and surface functional groups of CFCDs were identified through FTIR and XPS. As shown in the FTIR spectrum of CFCDs (Fig. 1f), the broad band at 3600–3100 cm-1 is ascribed to OH and NH stretching vibrations. The stretching vibration of the CH bond exhibits a small band at 2927 cm−1. Moreover, the absorption peaks centered at 1571 cm−1 and 1387 cm−1 are assigned to the symmetric and antisymmetric vibrations of COO, respectively, which is an indication of the presence of carboxylic acid or the functional groups containing oxygen [36,37]. The survey XPS spectrum of CFCDs (Fig. 2 a) showed three obvious bonding energy peaks at 285.29 eV, 532.29 eV, and 399.29 eV that can be assigned to C1s, O1s, and N1s, respectively, indicating that elemental carbon (37.09 %), oxygen (54.33 %), and nitrogen (0.77 %), are mainly involved in the composition of CFCDs [38]. Three peaks at 284.67 eV, 286.26 eV, and 288.0 eV in the deconvoluted spectrum of C1s (Fig. 2b) can be assigned to CC/CC, C-OH/CN, and COC/CO, respectively [39]. As shown in Fig. 2c, the high-resolution N1s spectrum of CFCDs showed two peaks, the CNC peak (399.45 eV) and the N-C3 peak (400.15 eV) [20,40]. The O1s band of CFCDs (Fig. 2d) could be deconvoluted into two Gaussian peaks associated with COC/C-OH and CO, situated at 531.78 eV and 532.9 eV, respectively [41]. The information on the surface functional groups of the CFCDs obtained from XPS and FTIR spectra indicated that many hydrophilic functional groups exist on the surface of CFCDs, such as OH and COOH, which are beneficial for enhancing the solubility and stability of CFCDs in aqueous systems [42].

Colloidal stability and photostability are indispensable factors for the application of CFCDs in various fields. To reveal the colloidal stability of CFCDs, their zeta potential at a pH of 7.0 was measured. Owing to the abundant oxygen-containing groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl, etc.) in CFCDs, a negative zeta potential of −31.13 ± 1.36 mV was observed (Fig. S2), which guarantees its excellent water dispersibility and long-term stability in aqueous solutions [43], so CFCDs remained a homogeneous solution for a long time without obvious aggregation. Furthermore, there was no discernible change in the FL intensity of the CFCDs for 30 days (Fig. S3), which illustrates that the CFCDs have outstanding stability during long-term storage. In addition, Fig. S4 shows that the FL intensity of the CFCDs barely changes after 4 h of continuous irradiation with a UV lamp at 365 nm, indicating the strong resistance to photobleaching of CFCDs.

pH and ionic strength stability are also pivotal prerequisites for CFCDs to be used in the biomedical field because CFCDs are required to remain stable in the body’s physiological environment which contains a potentially broad pH and ionic range [44]. The ionic strength tolerance of CFCDs was determined by measuring its FL intensity at different NaCl concentrations. The FL intensity of the CFCDs (Fig. 3 a) changed only slightly with increasing NaCl concentration (0–1 M), suggesting that CFCDs are suitable for practical applications at different salt concentrations [45]. The cellular environment is mostly maintained at a neutral to slightly alkaline pH, while the tumor cell microenvironment and a few organelles, such as lysosomes and endosomes, are maintained at an acidic pH (approximately 5–6) [46,47]. The stability of CFCDs under different pH environments was also evaluated, and some interesting phenomena were observed. Fig. 3b shows that CFCDs maintain a stable and strong FL intensity over a pH range of 5–8, demonstrating their good stability under physiological conditions. These results illustrated that CFCDs can potentially be applied in the field of biomedicine.

Notably, the FL intensity of CFCDs increases from 1326 a.u. to 2208 a.u. as the pH changed from 2 to 7, while only slightly decreased in the pH range of 7–12 (from 2208 a.u. to 1839 a.u.) (Fig. 3b), which elucidates that the FL intensity of the CFCDs has some pH dependent behavior.

To evaluate the potential of CFCDs as a pH sensor, their response properties in the pH range of 0.5–4.5 were further explored. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the FL intensity of the CFCDs increased progressively with increasing pH, while the position of the FL emission peak remained the same. In addition, the FL intensity of the CFCDs increases linearly with pH value from 0.5 to 4.5 according to the linear equation as Y = 172.21X + 596.62 (R2 = 0.9937, X: pH value, Y: the FL intensity of CFCDs, Fig. 4b), consequently the CFCDs are expected to be used as a new type of nanoprobe for pH sensing under extreme acidic conditions. Furthermore, the FL reversibility of the CFCDs was detected, and the FL intensity of the CFCDs remained almost constant when pH was reciprocally switched between 0.5 and 4.5 for three cycles (Fig. 4c), demonstrating that CFCDs have good reversibility and hold promise as a nanoprobe for “off-on-off” pH measurement [48]. The comparison of CFCDs with previously reported pH fluorescent probes based on CDs was performed in Table S1, from which it can be seen that CFCDs are exactly a rare pH sensor for extremely acidic condition.

To reveal the possible mechanism of the pH-dependent behavior of CFCDs, their zeta potentials obtained at different pH values (1–10) were investigated. Fig. S5 shows that as the pH value decreased from 6 to 1, the zeta potential of the CFCDs decreased rapidly from −28.2 mV to −8.0 mV, while the potential of the CFCDs did not change when the pH increased from 6 to 10, which is in accordance with the pH-response trend of its FL intensity (Fig. S6). This phenomenon is probably ascribed to the protonation and deprotonation of the oxygenated functional groups (including OH and COOH) that are abundant on the surface of the CFCDs [49]. Specifically, under acidic conditions, the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of CFCDs are protonated in the presence of large amounts of H+ with the transformation from COO− and O− to COOH and OH, which will result in a decrease in their zeta potential with the decreasing pH value [50]. With the decrease of zeta potential, the mutual repulsive force between particles is consequently weakened, and the colloidal stability of CFCDs is generally reduced, which makes them tend to aggregate in aqueous solutions. As a result, the fluorescence intensity of CFCD exhibits a gradual decrease under acidic conditions due to the aggregation-induced emission quenching (AIQ) effect [51,52]. In contrast, with the increase of pH, the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of CFCDs are deprotonated and present as COO− and O− under neutral and basic conditions, which provide the CFCDs with sufficient negative charge on their surface to maintain their colloidal stability in aqueous [53]. As shown in Fig. S7, after standing for a period of time, CFCD solutions at pH 1–4 generated a little precipitation at the bottom, and the precipitation gradually increased with decreasing pH, while no precipitation was observed in CFCD solutions at pH 5–10. This phenomenon is well responsive to the pH-dependent FL properties of CFCDs in the pH range of 0.5–4.5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and cell imaging of CFCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The viability of cells was used to assess the cytotoxicity of the CFCDs after treatment with CFCDs at different concentrations (0.4–2.0 mg/mL). As illustrated in Fig. 5 , the cell viabilities of MDA-MB-231 cells and MCF-7 cells were unaffected by increasing concentrations of CFCDs, with cell viability exceeding 90 % at all concentrations. The cell viability of Caco-2 cells gradually decreased with increasing concentrations of CFCDs; however, even at a concentration of 2.0 mg/mL, the cell viability was still >85 %. The above results demonstrate that CFCDs exhibit excellent biocompatibility with minimal toxicity.

Encouraged by the excellent stability and biocompatibility of CFCDs, the imaging performance of CFCDs toward living cells was subsequently assessed. Bright green FL was observed in Caco-2, MDA-MB-231, and MCF-7 cells under 305 nm laser excitation (Fig. 6 ), indicating that CFCDs can label these cells homogeneously. From the merged CLSM images, the FL signal was mainly observed in the cytoplasm, demonstrating that CFCDs were internalized by the cell through nonspecific phagocytosis and still maintained its FL properties in the cells [54]. These results suggest that CFCDs with excellent stability and biocompatibility can potentially be applied as biological imaging reagents.

BT and CT were measured through tail bleeding tests and blood coagulation tests, respectively, to evaluate the hemostatic effect of CFCDs. The hemostatic response includes vascular repair, platelet plug formation, and coagulation. BT is an index for a comprehensive evaluation of the hemostatic ability of mice, especially the vascular system and platelet function, while CT is an important indicator of intrinsic coagulation pathway function [55]. In addition, YNBY, a hemostatic drug commonly used in clinical practice, was selected as a positive reference to evaluate the hemostatic activity of CFCDs [56]. As shown in Fig. 7 , the BT of the positive group (9.3 ± 2.7 min) and the CFCD-treated groups at low, middle, and high doses (8.4 ± 1.6, 7.5 ± 1.5, 6.8 ± 1.3 min, respectively) were significantly lower than that of the control group (19.0 ± 2.4 min, p < 0.01); similarly, the CT values of the positive group and the CFCD groups with different doses were significantly lower compared with that in the control group (p < 0.01), which decreased by 27.43 %, 30.54 %, 51.92 %, and 57.87 %. Moreover, the BT values of the high-dose CFCD group as well as the CT values of the CFCD groups at middle and high doses were significantly lower than those in the positive control group (p < 0.05). These results demonstrate that CFCDs exhibit a good dose-dependent hemostatic effect, and thus are a promising hemostatic drug for the clinical treatment of bleeding diseases.

To determine the underlying mechanism of the hemostatic effect of CFCDs, the PT, APTT, TT, and platelet aggregation rate were measured in vitro. PT is an important indicator to assess the activity of the extrinsic pathway, and the levels of coagulation factors V, VII, and X in plasma are the main influencing factors; while APTT is related to the intrinsic pathway in plasma and can reflect the levels of coagulation factors VIII, IX, XI, and XII in plasma; TT is used to assess the process of fibrinogen to fibrin conversion, reflecting the common coagulation pathway [28]. As shown in Fig. 8 , after treatment with CFCDs, PT and TT gradually decreased with increasing CFCD concentration, and compared with the blank control, CFCDs significantly decreased PT at concentrations of 90 and 180 μg/mL and significantly reduced TT at all concentrations (p < 0.05), suggesting that the hemostatic effect of CFCDs is probably connected to the extrinsic pathway and the common pathway. However, different CFCD concentrations had no effect on APTT and were not significantly different from NS (p > 0.05), indicating that the hemostatic effect of CFCDs is unrelated to the endogenous hemostatic pathway.

Thrombosis has a central role in normal hemostasis, and platelet aggregation is the primary mechanism mediating thrombus formation and platelet adhesion [57]. Compared with the control group (60.98 %), the platelet aggregation rate was significantly increased after treatment with different concentrations of CFCDs (67.95 %–73.31 %, p < 0.01), indicating that CFCDs can have a hemostatic effect by promoting the aggregation of platelets in blood. The possible explanation is that the CFCDs with negatively charged surfaces activate the coagulation cascade, and thus promoting platelet activation; besides, the abundant carboxyl groups on the surface of CFCDs also enhance their interfacial interactions with blood cells [58,59]. In conclusion, the hemostatic effect of CFCDs is due to the promotion of platelet aggregation and activation of the extrinsic and common coagulation pathways.

The excellent hemostatic effect of CFCDs confirms our conjecture that CFCDs process biological activities similar to those of the traditional herbal medicine CFC, which was prepared by high-temperature carbonization, demonstrating that using herbal medicines as precursors is an effective strategy to obtain CDs that maintain their original biological activity. Similarly, Zhao et al. [60] prepared Paeoniae Radix Alba (PRA)-derived CDs with hepatoprotective effects. The charcoal-processed product of PRA, the blood-nourishing traditional Chinese medicine jujube, was used as a precursor to synthesize CDs and could stimulate erythroid progenitor cell self-renewal to specifically promote erythroid cell proliferation [61]. In brief, these results will provide some beneficial references for discovering multifunctional CDs from herbal medicines.

In this study, multifunctional CFCDs were successfully synthesized by pyrolysis method using the Chinese herbal medicine CF as a precursor. The obtained CFCDs not only exhibit high hydrophilicity, excellent chemical stability and photostability, but also have pH-sensitive FL properties based on proton transfer with good linearity in the pH range from 0.5 to 4.5, enabling an “off-on-off” pH monitoring function under extremely acidic conditions. In addition, the outstanding biocompatibility of CFCDs gives them great application potential in biomedical fields such as in live cell imaging. More importantly, CFCDs can exert good hemostatic effects through extrinsic pathway, common pathway, and can promote platelet aggregation. Taken together, CFCDs are expected to integrate multiple functions, such as pH sensing, cell imaging, and hemostasis, into one, which will supply great convenience for their application in various medical fields.

Rui Tian: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Yufan Guo: Validation, Investigation. Fazhen Luo: Validation, Investigation. Tianshu Yang: Investigation. Zhenhua Zhou: Resources. Zhengtao Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Yan Xie: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (81873198) and the Academic Leader Program of Shanghai Municipal Health Commission (2022XD026).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2022.114531.
